elements.2412-15297_sent_61487d1c: \title{Enhancing the detection of low-energy M dwarf flares: wavelet-based denoising of CHEOPS data} \subtitle{} \author{J.
elements.2412-15297_sent_13edc75f: Ribas\inst{2,4} \href{https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6689-0312}{\protect\includegraphics[height=0.25cm]{Fig/orcid.pdf}} \institute{Departament de Física Quàntica i Astrofísica, Institut de Ciències del Cosmos (ICCUB), Universitat de Barcelona (IEEC-UB), Martí i Franquès 1, E-08028 Barcelona, Spain\\ \email{julienpoyatos@icc.ub.edu} \and Institut d'Estudis Espacials de Catalunya (IEEC), 08860 Castelldefels (Barcelona), Spain \and Departament d’Enginyeria Electrònica i Biom{\`e}dica, Institut de Ciències del Cosmos (ICCUB), Universitat de Barcelona, IEEC-UB, Martí i Franquès 1, E-08028 Barcelona, Spain \and Institut de Ci{\`e}ncies de l’Espai (ICE, CSIC), Campus UAB, c/de Can Magrans s/n, E-08193 Bellaterra, Barcelona, Spain \date{Received XX YY, ZZ; accepted XX YY, ZZ} \abstract {Stellar flares are powerful bursts of electromagnetic radiation triggered by magnetic reconnection in the chromosphere of stars, occurring frequently and intensely on active M dwarfs.
elements.2412-15297_sent_94e17eea: While missions like TESS and Kepler have studied regular and super-flares, their detection of flares with energies below $10^{30}$ erg remains incomplete.
elements.2412-15297_sent_485f566e: Extending flare studies to include these low-energy events could enhance flare formation models and provide insight into their potential impacts on exoplanetary atmospheres.} {This study investigates the capacity of CHEOPS to detect low-energy flares in M dwarf light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_cdfa1f81: Leveraging CHEOPS’s high photometric precision and observing cadence, along with a tailored wavelet-based denoising algorithm, we aim to improve detection completeness and refine flare statistics for low-energy events.} {We conducted a flare injection and recovery process to optimise denoising parameters, applied it to CHEOPS light curves to maximise flare detection rates, and used a flare breakdown algorithm to analyse complex flare structures.} {Our analysis recovered 349 flares with energies ranging from $2.2 \times 10^{26}$ to $8.1 \times 10^{30}$ erg across 63 M dwarfs, with $\sim$40\% exhibiting complex, multi-peaked structures.
elements.2412-15297_sent_e70922b4: The denoising algorithm improved the overall flare recovery by $\sim$34\%, although it only marginally extended the lower boundary of detectable energies.
elements.2412-15297_sent_0305b691: For the full sample, the power-law index $\alpha$ was $1.92 \pm 0.07$, but a log-normal distribution provided a slightly better fit, suggesting multiple possible flare formation scenarios.} {Observing low-energy flares requires high photometric precision and fast observing cadence.
elements.2412-15297_sent_649ddc9c: Although CHEOPS’s on-target observing mode is not optimal for large-scale flare surveys, it can capture weaker flares than TESS and Kepler, extending the observed energy range.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f03e456d: Wavelet-based denoising further improves low-energy event recovery, enabling exploration of the upper end of the micro-flaring regime.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9dd64a4c: Expanding low-energy flare observations could refine flare generation models and improve our understanding of their role in star-planet interactions.} \keywords{Magnetic reconnection, Instrumentation: detectors, Methods: data analysis, Stars: activity, Stars: flare, Stars: low-mass} \maketitle
elements.2412-15297_sent_34e49a3a: Stellar flares are stochastic and intense bursts of electromagnetic radiation and charged particles triggered by magnetic reconnection in the chromosphere of stars.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7273b836: This phenomenon occurs when magnetic field lines in the chromosphere realign, releasing energy across the electromagnetic spectrum.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3d7a0995: This can generate flares of varying intensity and duration, significantly increasing stellar luminosity \citep{Benz_2010}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_e51472be: The star's magnetic activity significantly influences the frequency and energy of stellar flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_e51be3fd: M dwarfs are known for their high levels of magnetic activity and for flaring more frequently than earlier-type stars.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d1f3091e: This is due to their more vigorous dynamo processes yielding a higher incidence of magnetic reconnection events \citep{Kowalski_2024}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d513c7d6: Such stars can experience more frequent and energetic flares, sometimes reaching energy levels that are several orders of magnitude more intense than solar flares \citep{Bruevich_2001,Howard_2018}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d15dd550: The effects of stellar flares on planetary habitability are various.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4af0e2dc: On the one hand, stellar flares can damage planetary atmospheres: X-ray and ultraviolet radiation, along with charged particles emitted during flares, can deplete atmospheric layers, especially on planets with weak magnetic fields or near their host star.
elements.2412-15297_sent_dbb3bca5: This atmospheric erosion can compromise the potential for habitability by reducing the protection against harmful radiation and altering the planetary climate \citep{Ribas_2016,Airapetian_217}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_260afa71: On the other hand, the energy radiated during stellar flares may play a role in triggering prebiotic chemical processes: the increased ultraviolet radiation during flares can trigger chemical reactions that are crucial for the emergence of life, suggesting that flares may be necessary for prebiotic chemistry to happen on planets orbiting M dwarfs \citep{Scalo_2007,Rimmer_2018}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4fbd0ed7: Understanding the formation mechanisms, frequency, and energy distribution of stellar flares is essential for several reasons.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a7cd17c4: It provides insights into stellar magnetic activity and its variations across spectral types, especially in comparison to our Sun.
elements.2412-15297_sent_e4cdf532: Additionally, by investigating the flare activity of various stellar types, it is possible to refine models of stellar and planetary evolution \citep{Gudel_2007,Lammer_2012}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_0b24385f: TESS and Kepler have provided valuable data on flare occurrences, from cadences of minutes to a few tens of seconds.
elements.2412-15297_sent_44d2f76d: The 20-second cadence of TESS revealed that complex flare structures are frequent, including multi-peak profiles and quasi-periodic pulsations (QPPs).
elements.2412-15297_sent_4ce2aab4: These complex structures suggest that such non-classical flare shapes may result from emission cascades within a single active region or sympathetic flares from neighboring regions \citep{Davenport_2016,Howard_2022}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_abfe8997: In this context, the study of smaller-scale flare events becomes increasingly important to better characterise stellar variability and the formation mechanisms of flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f1331f22: Extending photometric precision and observing cadence down to a few seconds could greatly improve the understanding of these phenomena, potentially revealing even finer flare structures.
elements.2412-15297_sent_dfeed0d5: This increased sensitivity may enable the detection of micro- and nano-flares, small-scale flaring events with energies ranging from $10^{22}$ to $10^{27}$ erg that are believed to play a crucial role in coronal heating \citep{Parker_1988}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7d3a52da: However, no concluding evidence supports micro- and nano-flares against other possible explanations, mainly due to the difficulty of gathering precise statistics about these flares since they are hidden in the instrumental noise.
elements.2412-15297_sent_e86df09d: As such, it is still unclear if micro- and nano-flares follow the same trends as more powerful flaring events.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6beee01b: Furthermore, flare formation scenarios diverging from self-organised criticality propose that flares could consist of an avalanche of several combined micro- and nano-flares, which would have implications on the current understanding of the impact of flares on exoplanetary atmospheres \citep{Audard2000,Sheikh_2016,Aschwanden_2019}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_27c3e1c7: The CHaracterising ExOPlanet Satellite (CHEOPS) is a space telescope primarily designed to measure exoplanet transits, offering exceptional photometric precision.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a511a2ea: With a precision of approximately 20 ppm over a 6-hour integration period for a V$\sim$9 star, and down to 150 ppm for shorter timescales, CHEOPS surpasses other photometric instruments in the visible spectrum, such as TESS and current ground-based telescopes \citep{Benz_2021,Oddo_2023}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_399c9674: CHEOPS's observing cadence can also reach 0.001 seconds for very bright stars (V$\sim$6), several orders of magnitude faster than those of TESS and Kepler \citep{Borucki_2010,Ricker_2015}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2569e841: Although CHEOPS is limited to on-target observations, it provides high photometric precision and accessible observing cadence, which are crucial for capturing the short and stochastic nature of low-energy flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_76243a7b: To enhance the detection of flares in CHEOPS light curves, denoising techniques based on the wavelet transform can filter out noise from the signal. The wavelet transform decomposes time series data into different frequency components, facilitating the isolation and reduction of noise.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b3329dc3: Unlike the Fourier transform, which only provides frequency characteristics of the signal, the wavelet transform recovers both temporal and frequency information, making it more efficient in analysing transient signals like stellar flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_bfd9111d: The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) is commonly used as a base for time series denoising algorithms, with many applications outside of astronomy \citep{Pasti_1999,Polat_2018,Jang_2021}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_72bb6dc9: The main downside of the DWT is that it introduces by definition a downsampling of the input signal, which can lead to a loss of details due to its non-redundant nature \citep{Dohono_1994}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2a2e0cdb: In contrast, the Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT) avoids this by upsampling the wavelet coefficients at each decomposition level.
elements.2412-15297_sent_46f375b5: Although it involves a higher computational cost, the SWT provides the advantage of maintaining the original length of the signal at each decomposition level and providing a redundant, shift-invariant representation of the data \citep{Rhif_2019,Kumar_2021}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a0dafd0d: In the context of astronomy, techniques based on the SWT have been developed for noise filtering and demonstrated significant improvements in signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) and the ability to recover faint astrophysical signals \citep{Strack_2006}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_fe7d55f6: These methods emphasise the importance of adapting the current wavelet-based denoising techniques to the specific characteristics of astronomical time series and show that such denoising algorithms could enhance the detection of faint transient phenomena like low-energy flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_00737b7d: An efficient wavelet-based denoising algorithm is crucial for optimising the extraction of meaningful flare statistics in our analysis pipeline using CHEOPS data.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9c6a61ff: In this study, we explore the performance of the combination of CHEOPS's photometric precision and a SWT-based denoising algorithm in enhancing flare detection in M dwarf light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_01ab24a9: In Section \ref{section:methods}, we describe the target selection, the data reduction, the SWT parameter exploration, the flare detection, and flare breakdown algorithms.
elements.2412-15297_sent_64e33343: In Section \ref{section:results}, we quantify the improvement in flare recovery achieved by denoising, analyse the obtained flare catalogs, and compare our results to the literature.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a999a327: In Section \ref{section:discussion}, we discuss the limitations of this study and suggest several avenues for improvement.
elements.2412-15297_sent_514e601a: Among the data gathered by CHEOPS since the start of its operations, several observational programs have targeted late-type stars.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b1f37e1b: Pagano) is a prominent program that has been actively investigating the variability of late-K and M dwarf stars since the beginning of scientific observations in April 2020.
elements.2412-15297_sent_bbf250ba: This program is particularly focused on characterising the photometric variability of these stars, including flares and star spots.
elements.2412-15297_sent_988918ca: G Szabó) operated from July 2020 to December 2023.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7687c047: While its primary aim was to study the debris disks around various spectral types, this program included observations of several late-type stars to explore the interaction between stellar activity and circumstellar dust.
elements.2412-15297_sent_1196ece5: Szabó) was another significant program, running from August 2022 to September 2023, and specifically targeted the young M dwarf star AU Mic.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7ba78214: Known for its strong stellar activity and prominent debris disk, AU Mic is an ideal subject for examining the correlation between stellar flares and its surrounding environment.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4d623144: The nominal observation mode of CHEOPS produces subarrays every few tens of seconds, which results from the coaddition of multiple shorter exposures on board the spacecraft.
elements.2412-15297_sent_ea81cdcc: These intermediate images, called imagettes, are retained and can be used to construct light curves with a significantly higher cadence.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5313673e: The observer can adjust the imagettes exposure time based on the target's brightness, with exposure times reaching as short as 0.001 seconds for the brightest targets.
elements.2412-15297_sent_59af17dc: All the programs previously mentioned, however, chose to observe their respective targets at a minimum cadence of 3 seconds to optimise the actual on-target time, the telescope duty cycle and to find a compromise between SNR and saturation \citep{Boldog_2023,Bruno_2024}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f843bcb0: This cadence is still advantageous for detecting and analysing rapid, transient events like stellar flares, and provides a higher cadence than the 20-second cadence of TESS.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7230440e: To take advantage of the high precision and fast observing cadence provided by CHEOPS, we retrieved all publicly available imagettes data on main sequence M dwarfs from the CHEOPS archive\footnote{\url{https://cheops-archive.astro.unige.ch/archive_browser/}}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_26602f2c: Due to the proprietary period associated with CHEOPS programs, imagettes data are not yet available for all observations.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f6f3786b: Therefore, we completed our sample with the raw light curves provided in \cite{Bruno_2024}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7a170a48: In total, we obtained 66.65 days of on-target time from the three programs, comprising observations of 110 different M dwarfs.
elements.2412-15297_sent_23f0ce46: For each target, we retrieved the Gaia G band magnitude ($G_{\mathrm{mag}}$) and effective temperature ($T_{\mathrm{eff}}$) from the CHEOPS file metadata.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4f05c82e: We searched for $H_{\alpha}$ equivalent width values from \cite{Schofer_2019}, as well as log $R'_{HK}$ values from \cite{Astudillo_2017} and \cite{Boro_2018}, but found $H_{\alpha}$ EW values for only 64 of the 110 stars and log $R'_{HK}$ values for 71 stars.
elements.2412-15297_sent_11a96d64: This limited availability prevented us from providing a comprehensive activity indicator distribution for the entire sample.
elements.2412-15297_sent_94774e20: Instead, we used the mean rotational velocity ($V$\,sin\,$i$) values from Table A.1 of \cite{Bruno_2024} as an alternative indicator of stellar activity.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3ec60f32: We calculated the distance to each target using its parallax from Gaia DR3, or Gaia DR2 if not available.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2d1b215b: We obtained the radius of each star from the TESS Input Catalog.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4dda0e9c: The parameter distributions in our sample are shown in Figure \ref{fig:star_description}, and the full list is available in Table~\ref{target_list}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7835ccfe: As expected from the focus of the three observing programs, our sample is heavily biased towards AU Mic, with more than 340 orbits observed, while all other stars have been observed for fewer than 70 orbits each.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4d600e98: The CHEOPS Data Reduction Pipeline (DRP) does not automatically process imagettes data sets \citep{Hoyer_2020}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7d817acd: We hence relied on PIPE\footnote{\url{https://github.com/alphapsa/PIPE}}, a photometric extraction package developed by the CHEOPS consortium to complement the official DRP, to reduce imagettes and extract Point Spread Function (PSF) photometry \citep{Brandeker_2024}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_dfe1f501: We optimised the PSF parameters for each target by allowing the software to freely explore the impact of the radius of the fitting region, the number of components for the fit, a simultaneous fit for the background, dark current correction, and static image removal through a gradient-like search algorithm.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f27b822d: In the case of the light curves recovered by \cite{Bruno_2024}, this imagettes reduction was already performed using an ad hoc tool, the Data Reduction Tool (DRT), described in \cite{Morgado_2022} and \cite{Fortier_2024}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a65ae0be: We consistently found marginal differences of less than 50 ppm when comparing raw light curves extracted through both PIPE and the DRT.
elements.2412-15297_sent_e73fa3f4: We then employed the package PyCHEOPS\footnote{\url{https://github.com/pmaxted/pycheops}} \citep{Maxted_2022,Maxted_2023}, a tool designed by the CHEOPS consortium, to detrend CHEOPS photometric data.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f1d82a0c: We detrended all light curves by removing linear, quadratic, and cubic flux correlations against time, centroid position, sine and cosine of the roll angle, background flux, contamination, and smear.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2ca1bdb0: Finally, we normalised the light curves with a 2-degree polynomial to eliminate any long-term trends that could be attributed to stellar activity cycles.
elements.2412-15297_sent_8a6208d8: After visual inspection, we removed light curves that exhibited excessively high RMS values higher than 10,000 ppm, likely resulting from a failed photometric extraction and/or detrending, which corresponded to $\sim$6\% of the sample.
elements.2412-15297_sent_65c1cb31: 84\% of the resulting light curves were observed at a cadence of 3 seconds, 14\% between 5 and 7 seconds, and the remaining 2\% at a cadence higher than 12 seconds.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b537fa8c: Since the next steps of the analysis imply a denoising process, we refer in the rest of the paper to the detrended and normalised CHEOPS light curves as 'original' (as opposed to 'denoised').
elements.2412-15297_sent_e2d45ca9: CHEOPS observations are composed of multiple orbits, each lasting approximately 99 minutes.
elements.2412-15297_sent_98fcfe2a: The science time per orbit, however, is reduced and varies due to interruptions from the South Atlantic Anomaly or Earth occultations.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2dfd76e3: As a result, the number of usable data points can be reduced if the target is in a region difficult to observe by CHEOPS, while targets located near the ecliptic plane can benefit from longer uninterrupted observations \citep{Benz_2021}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7584536f: To indicate the actual science time composing our sample, we compile in Table \ref{tab:stars_distrib} the number of stars for each spectral subtype, the corresponding number of orbits, and the total on-target time.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b23dd785: A flare candidate cannot be considered as such if it spans two separate orbits because data points belonging to the flare profile would be missing from the resulting light curve.
elements.2412-15297_sent_0d6a5e02: Recovering only part of a flare would lead to underestimating the flare energy and biasing the derived flare statistics.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f3c3aee8: Therefore, our flare detection algorithm will only consider flares whose start and stop times occurred within the same orbit.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f9a02da1: Figure \ref{fig:orbit_durations} shows the distribution of the duration of the orbits constituting our sample.
elements.2412-15297_sent_49fa01b1: Although most of the expected orbits are shorter than 99 minutes, we found a few orbits with longer durations of up to 5 hours corresponding to targets easily observable by CHEOPS.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6cc1e500: Since M dwarf flares can have durations of up to $\sim$10 hours \citep{Davenport_2016,Pietras_2022}, conducting flare detection with CHEOPS implies that the population of long-duration flares will not be recovered in this study.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9fc92154: The photometric signature of flares is typically characterised by a sharp rise (usually associated with bremsstrahlung radiation) followed by an exponential decay (which corresponds to radiative cooling) \citep{Kowalski_2013,Davenport_2014}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6773ac0c: An effective way to detect flares is by monitoring the increase in flux they produce in the star's light curve.
elements.2412-15297_sent_54511e0d: AltaiPony\footnote{\url{https://altaipony.readthedocs.io/en/latest/}} is a software developed to automate the detection of flares in Kepler, K2, and TESS light curves by using a classical sigma-clipping algorithm \citep{Davenport_2016,Ilin_2022}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_444af355: It follows the flare detection algorithm defined by \cite{Chang_2015} by using three detection criteria: $N_1$, the minimum positive flux deviation from the median normalised by local scatter; $N_2$, accounting for photometric errors; and $N_3$, the minimum number of consecutive data points meeting these conditions.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7867352b: Typically, $N_1 = 3$, $N_2 = N_1 - 1$, and $N_3 = 3$ are used, corresponding to flare durations of 3 minutes for Kepler and 1 minute for TESS \citep{Davenport_2016,Ilin_2021}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_945deb33: For CHEOPS, we adjusted the criteria to $N_1 = 3$, $N_2 = 2$, and $N_3 = 5$, requiring a minimum of 5 consecutive points above the $3\sigma$ threshold.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5f3d3cbc: While this allows us to reduce the number of false positive detections, it also excludes flares shorter than 15 seconds, limiting the detection of the most rapid events.
elements.2412-15297_sent_71de69e6: A consequence of using a sigma-clipping method for flare detection is that the noise profile of the CHEOPS instrument sets the threshold for the minimum amplitude of flares that can be detected above the noise floor.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3bc57839: By denoising the CHEOPS light curves with a wavelet-based algorithm, we aim to lower this noise floor while preserving the amplitude of flare events, thereby enhancing the SNR of low-energy flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_acd43074: This approach is expected to increase the recovery rate of flares that previously fell just below the detection threshold, thus allowing them to meet the detection criteria more consistently.
elements.2412-15297_sent_624f0821: Following this idea, the specific workflow of this study is the following:
elements.2412-15297_sent_27b588ea: Determine the noise profile of a 'prototypical' M dwarf light curve observed by CHEOPS.
elements.2412-15297_sent_90e46d66: Generate a corresponding synthetic light curve in which to inject flares of different energies, durations, and amplitudes.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9e36beb1: Determine the denoising parameters that optimise the recovery of the injected flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b70451ac: Apply the wavelet-based denoising algorithm with the optimal parameters on the original CHEOPS light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_ed7469d8: Conduct flare detection on the original and denoised CHEOPS light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a7e3c3d2: A flow diagram of the entire procedure followed in this study is presented in Figure \ref{fig:procedure}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b2a6ee92: \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{Fig/procedure.pdf} \caption{Flow diagram of the procedure followed in this paper.} \end{figure*}
elements.2412-15297_sent_ca40043e: To determine the typical noise profile of a CHEOPS light curve, we computed the average noise profile for each spectral subtype by averaging the photometric fluctuations from the median value of each light curve in our sample, following the method of \cite{Jess_2019,Dillon_2020} and \cite{Grant_2023}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_73242ced: We chose to discard the M6V subtype since only one star was included in this group (see Table \ref{tab:stars_distrib}).
elements.2412-15297_sent_68d32fe0: Therefore this study comprises targets of spectral type ranging from M0V to M5V.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3e3affa6: We display in Figure \ref{fig:noises} the fluctuations for each spectral subtype and compare them against a normal distribution.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c646f61e: The resulting noise distributions for each spectral subtype are noticeably thinner and higher than a normal distribution and have more pronounced tails, indicating a leptokurtic nature.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d4923071: This is confirmed by the Q-Q plots for each spectral subtype on the second row of Figure \ref{fig:noises}, where the noise distributions exhibit heavier tails than the normal distribution (see Figure 3 of~\cite{Jess_2019} for a collection of different distribution types).
elements.2412-15297_sent_f5466d34: A leptokurtic noise distribution has several implications for the light curves and the flare detection.
elements.2412-15297_sent_8c61d580: First, the presence of heavier tails means that there are more extreme fluctuations than expected in a normal distribution, which could mimic or obscure low-amplitude flare events, complicating their detection.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a442d7ae: This distribution shape suggests that the photometric precision of the CHEOPS instrument is not purely Gaussian and contains elements that contribute to a higher frequency of outliers.
elements.2412-15297_sent_db2640b2: Consequently, the denoising process must account accurately for these leptokurtic features to efficiently distinguish between data points caused by flares and those caused by noise artifacts.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a6a6ebfd: We compile in Table \ref{Table:noise_parameters} the mean value, standard deviation, skewness, and Fisher kurtosis of the noise distribution corresponding to each spectral subtype.
elements.2412-15297_sent_73890af8: By understanding the specific noise characteristics, we can refine the denoising and flare detection algorithm to reduce the number of false positives and improve the reliability of flare detection.
elements.2412-15297_sent_8706a145: On the top row, the red dashed curves correspond to a normal distribution, while the black histograms correspond to the noise distribution histograms in the CHEOPS light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2327292d: On the bottom row, the red dashed lines correspond to the quantiles of a normal distribution, while the black curves correspond to the quantiles of the noise distribution in the CHEOPS light curves.} \end{figure*}

elements.2412-15297_sent_9d334159: The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) is commonly used to decompose and denoise time series data.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5babe65f: This is done by scaling and translating a mother wavelet across the data, resulting in a decomposition into different frequency bands through low-pass and high-pass filtering, followed by a downsampling.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6f983ef6: Stochastic noise, typically in high-frequency components, is thresholded and removed before reconstructing the data by using the inverse transform.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3b24f846: Unlike other denoising methods based on the Fourier Transform, the DWT retains both temporal and frequency information, which is beneficial for recovering non-stationary signals \citep{Mallat_2009}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_fc67fff7: However, the Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT) is often preferred for denoising as it avoids downsampling, maintaining the signal length at each decomposition level through an à trous algorithm.
elements.2412-15297_sent_15dbaeb1: This approach offers a redundant, shift-invariant representation that enhances noise reduction accuracy and effectively preserves signal features, resulting in more consistent and reliable denoising results \citep{Percival_2000,Fors_2006}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_1c764f93: The threshold value in wavelet-based denoising is key to distinguish between noise and signal by determining which wavelet coefficients to suppress or retain.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5959120a: A well-chosen threshold effectively reduces noise while preserving essential signal features.
elements.2412-15297_sent_044376b3: However, an overly high threshold can erase important details, while a low threshold may fail to remove enough noise.
elements.2412-15297_sent_cd03cbdf: Determining the threshold value typically involves statistical methods, such as the universal threshold proposed by \cite{Dohono_1994}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_baa15561: Its logarithmic scaling with data length ensures effective noise filtering for larger time series while preserving signal features \citep{Abramovich_1995,Percival_2000}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_950a0afa: We adapted this universal threshold by replacing its dependence on the signal's standard deviation by the median absolute deviation, which is more robust to outliers.
elements.2412-15297_sent_0022e266: This adjustment effectively balances noise reduction and signal preservation, especially in the presence of non-Gaussian noise \citep{DelSer_2018}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c7a28333: The decomposition level determines the number of times the data is iteratively decomposed into wavelet components, with higher levels providing finer resolution of the signal and stronger denoising.
elements.2412-15297_sent_101a9a19: Each decomposition level corresponds to different frequency scales in the data, enabling multi-resolution analysis that typically separates noise from significant signal features.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5f740d1e: As such, the decomposition level is generally chosen based on the number of data points in the input data.
elements.2412-15297_sent_bd831635: An appropriate decomposition level allows to effectively separate noise from the signal while preserving key signal characteristics.
elements.2412-15297_sent_0a92138f: A level too low may fail to remove enough noise, while a level too high may suppress important signal details and introduce artifacts from the mother wavelet function \citep{Yang_2016}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_402880e3: Since each decomposition level halves the data frequency, consequently capturing more detail at lower frequencies, wavelet decomposition follows a binary scaling \citep{Mallat_1989}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_bf6b2636: Therefore, the maximum number of useful decomposition levels $J$ scales logarithmically with the number of data points $N$, such that $2^J \leq N$.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a05ce801: This ensures effective noise isolation while preserving the signal's integrity \citep{Daubechies_1992}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6bf12c21: CHEOPS orbits vary in the number of data points based on orbit duration and observing cadence.
elements.2412-15297_sent_af07d73a: The decomposition level is tailored to the number of data points in each orbit, ensuring shorter orbits are decomposed at a lower level than longer ones.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6067406a: This optimisation improves the denoising process and ensures effective use of the SWT across the different orbit sizes in our sample.

elements.2412-15297_sent_8e9f1463: The recovered and lost flare candidates are highlighted with a green and red background, respectively.} \end{figure*} The choice of the mother wavelet is crucial in wavelet-based denoising, as it affects the accuracy of noise separation from the signal. Wavelets with short support, such as the Haar wavelet, are efficient at detecting sharp discontinuities but may introduce high-frequency noise, whereas wavelets with longer supports, like the Daubechies and Symlets, are better at capturing smooth variations but may blur sharp features \citep{Haar_1910,Mallat_1989,Daubechies_1992}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2424d619: Vanishing moments refer to a wavelet's ability to represent polynomials up to a certain degree.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b62c6742: Wavelets with higher vanishing moments are more effective at filtering noise but are computationally demanding and may lead to overfitting \citep{Dohono_1994}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d4a9701b: For the SWT, discrete and biorthogonal wavelets are used to ensure accurate data reconstruction, non-redundancy, and minimal introduction of artifacts \citep{Chui_1992}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7e8dd808: To identify the optimal parameters for denoising CHEOPS light curves, we followed a systematic approach, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:injections}:

elements.2412-15297_sent_8b61bac2: We separated flare peaks by 45 minutes to ensure that the flux had returned to a quiescent level before the beginning of the next flare.
elements.2412-15297_sent_186e5f0f: We injected a noise profile corresponding to each spectral subtype according to the distribution parameters compiled in Table \ref{Table:noise_parameters}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_aab79f21: We conducted a SWT-based denoising using the previously mentioned threshold for each discrete and biorthogonal mother wavelet included in the package PyWavelets \citep{Lee_2019}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7028cded: This included wavelets from the Haar (haar), Daubechies (db), Symlets (sym), Coiflets (coif), and Biorthogonal (bior) families, with decomposition levels from 1 to 8.

elements.2412-15297_sent_4d467657: Given their frequent use in analysing astrophysical datasets, we selected Daubechies wavelets for denoising the CHEOPS light curves \citep{Ojeda_2014,Souza_2018,Bolzan_2020}.

elements.2412-15297_sent_456f5b24: An important parameter of the SWT is the coefficient thresholding method.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b3f4b47c: Hard thresholding sets coefficients below the threshold to zero, preserving signal features but potentially introducing discontinuities and artifacts in the reconstructed signal. Soft thresholding, on the other hand, not only sets coefficients below the threshold to zero but also shrinks the remaining coefficients towards zero, resulting in smoother data with fewer artifacts.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c8b5d580: However, it can slightly attenuate preserved features.
elements.2412-15297_sent_33182d11: Generally, hard thresholding is better for retaining sharp features, while soft thresholding improves signal smoothness and continuity.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d6b30d12: To evaluate the impact of these thresholding methods, we used the db18 wavelet and a decomposition level of 2 to denoise the synthetic light curves with injected flares for each spectral subtype.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f62b1bd7: Hard and soft thresholding methods were applied, and the resulting recovery rates as a function of injected flare energy are displayed in Figure \ref{fig:percentages}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_115b7e35: The two methods allow to increase the detection completeness of low-energy flares, with only marginal differences in recovery rate between them.
elements.2412-15297_sent_fd4f9b58: Despite this, we chose to use a hard thresholding for denoising the CHEOPS light curves for several reasons.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c727403b: The signal of low-amplitude flares could be affected by the shrinkage introduced by soft thresholding, which would lead to the non-detection of the flares reaching just above the detection threshold.
elements.2412-15297_sent_edd55951: Additionally, the shrinkage could potentially lead to inaccurate energy estimations for detected flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_1d6d02e7: Finally, hard thresholding is less computationally intensive than soft thresholding, as it requires one less operation on each coefficient value, making it a more efficient method when applied to large datasets.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4c5445bb: Our flare detection algorithm essentially functions as a binary classifier, distinguishing between 'flaring' and 'non-flaring' states.
elements.2412-15297_sent_1db791bd: To evaluate its performance, we generated 1000 flare-free light curves for each $10^{25}$ erg energy bin and applied the detection algorithm to measure its classification efficiency.
elements.2412-15297_sent_540c4a2f: Specifically, we constructed confusion matrices for each injected flare energy bin, defining four possible outcomes: true positives (TP), where a flare was injected and correctly retrieved; false positives (FP), where no flare was injected but one was erroneously retrieved; false negative (FN), where a flare was injected but not retrieved; and true negatives (TN), where no flare was injected and none was retrieved.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9935d8e7: In Figure \ref{fig:confusion_matrices_evolution2}, we present the evolution of the confusion matrix metrics as a function of the injected flare energy, averaged across all spectral types, for both the original and denoised light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_bdbb27ee: We observed that the false positive rate (FPR) remained independent of the injected flare energy but instead depended on the noise level in the light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7f49b337: Notably, the FPR remained consistently low across the entire energy range: below 0.025 for non-denoised light curves and below 0.010 for denoised light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4631ef5b: The denoising process significantly enhanced the true positive rate (TPR), effectively shifting the detection threshold towards lower injected flare energies by nearly half an order of magnitude.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5847460e: To quantify these improvements, we identified four key thresholds:
elements.2412-15297_sent_8c845cbe: $t_1$: where the TPR for denoised light curves exceeds 10\% ($1.1\times10^{27}$ erg),
elements.2412-15297_sent_e4b073d3: $t_2$: where the TPR for original light curves exceeds 10\% ($3.5\times10^{27}$ erg),
elements.2412-15297_sent_cd0116c2: $t_3$: where the TPR for denoised light curves reaches 90\% ($1.1\times10^{28}$ erg),
elements.2412-15297_sent_2195625b: $t_4$: where the TPR for original light curves reaches 90\% ($4.4\times10^{28}$ erg).
elements.2412-15297_sent_70b226fd: Figure \ref{fig:confusion_matrices_tresholds} illustrates the confusion matrices at these thresholds.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b94ab36d: Consistent with the trend shown in Figure \ref{fig:percentages}, the denoising process significantly improves the TPR between $t_1$ and $t_4$, enabling a more complete detection of flares at lower energies compared to the original light curves.
elements.2412-15297_subsection_2b24fb54: Flare detection in denoised light curves
elements.2412-15297_sent_415cf55a: We then denoised all CHEOPS light curves (further referred to as 'denoised'), separated by orbits and performed the flare detection described in Section \ref{subsection:flare_detection}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_bb0461f0: Following the methodology of \citet{Raetz_2020,Bruno_2024} and \citet{Fortier_2024}, we determined the quiescent luminosity of each star in the sample by adopting the CHEOPS zero-point and effective wavelengths available on the filter profile service of the Spanish Virtual Observatory \citep{Rodrigo_2012} as follows:
elements.2412-15297_sent_037880b9: where $d$ is the distance of the star, $Q_{cheops}$ is the quantum efficiency of the CHEOPS sensor integrated over the 330-1100nm wavelength range, $F_{vega}$ is the CHEOPS flux of Vega (CHEOPS zero-point), and $G$ is the Gaia $G$ band magnitude of the star.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9f9a2fe8: We also derived several flare parameters, such as the flare duration and FWHM, flare impulse (amplitude / duration), and flare peak luminosity (amplitude $\times$ quiescent luminosity).
elements.2412-15297_sent_0550056b: To break down complex flares displaying several visible peaks, we consider that such events can be described as a superposition of several simple flares, which can be modeled by the empirical flare template of Llamaradas Estelares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_437295b6: For a given complex flare event, the objective is therefore to automatically determine the number of flares and their parameters (namely $t_{peak}$, amplitude, and FWHM) that best describe it.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d5807fe9: For this, we developed an algorithm to iteratively fit up to 5 flares for every flaring event detected using AltaiPony.
elements.2412-15297_sent_93b79a1c: The flare region to fit for each event included 15 seconds before the start time and 45 seconds after the stop time of the flare to ensure the flux fully returned to the quiescent level within the fitting window.
elements.2412-15297_sent_644abe7e: Following the procedure of \cite{Davenport_2014}, we imposed a first guess and boundaries for the fitted flare parameters.
elements.2412-15297_sent_52b194c3: We seeded the parameters of the fitting by using the peak flux amplitude and time and 15\% of the flare full duration for the FWHM.
elements.2412-15297_sent_54874191: Each component's relative amplitude was required to be larger than $10^{-4}$, being the flare amplitude after which the recovery rate dropped to 0\% across all spectral types (see Figure \ref{fig:recovery}).
elements.2412-15297_sent_bb3376f0: We also required $t_{peak}$ to occur within the boundaries of the flare window, and FWHM to be larger than the observing cadence of the light curve and smaller than 50\% of the flare total duration.
elements.2412-15297_sent_41254bd5: No priors on the relationships between flare amplitude, FWHM and $t_{peak}$ were included. Following \cite{Bruno_2024}, we used the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) to determine the best fit, which took the form:
elements.2412-15297_sent_091eceba: where M is the number of data points that fell within the flare window, and $k_n$ is the number of degrees of freedom of the $n$-th model.
elements.2412-15297_sent_62021c50: This statistic determines the improvement of the fit (decreased $\chi^2$) while penalising the increasing number of free parameters used in the model.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4f8327bf: We chose to use the AIC instead of the BIC like \cite{Davenport_2014} because the AIC penalises less the number of components.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3ad99143: As such, the AIC tends to favour more complex models with a higher number of parameters, which would enable further breakdown.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3bf16ce1: Finally, the best solution fit was selected as the $n$-th model with the smallest AIC to have decreased by at least 6 units from the previous $(n - 1)$th model.
elements.2412-15297_sent_cecde681: The choice of a 6-unit improvement threshold was determined in \cite{Bruno_2024} by visual inspection of complex flare events to ensure the algorithm did not overfit them.
elements.2412-15297_sent_94c70c3b: We began our analysis by applying the flare detection algorithm to the detrended CHEOPS light curves, initially identifying 260 flares with energies ranging from $4.3 \times 10^{26}$ erg to $8.1 \times 10^{30}$ erg.
elements.2412-15297_sent_80a42cdc: To optimise the detection process, we employed a SWT-based denoising algorithm using the db18 wavelet base, as detailed in Section \ref{subsection:denoising}, to reduce noise within each CHEOPS orbit.
elements.2412-15297_sent_735731a3: We then reran the flare detection algorithm on the denoised light curves and detected a total of 349 flares with energies ranging between $2.2 \times 10^{26}$ erg and $8.1 \times 10^{30}$ erg.
elements.2412-15297_sent_24f9cc27: After running the flare breakdown algorithm, 142 individual components were recovered from 61 complex events in the original light curves, with energies ranging from $1.7 \times 10^{27}$ erg to $4.5 \times 10^{30}$ erg.
elements.2412-15297_sent_81602147: 298 individual flare components were recovered in the denoised light curves from 123 complex events, with energies ranging from $1.8 \times 10^{26}$ erg to $4.1 \times 10^{30}$ erg.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c8d65307: The denoising process resulted in a significant increase in the overall flare recovery of $\sim$34\% and improved the recovery of individual components of complex flares by $\sim$110\%.
elements.2412-15297_sent_31e10036: Nine events (less than 2\% of the denoised flare sample) had recovered energies lower than $10^{27}$ erg, therefore belonging to the upper end of the micro-flare energy range.

elements.2412-15297_sent_b26b231c: This work made use of CHEOPS to study white-light flares in an unexplored region of the energy range.
elements.2412-15297_sent_bf79bc26: We employed a denoising algorithm based on the SWT to enhance the detection of low-energy flares in the light curves of a sample of M dwarfs.
elements.2412-15297_sent_cc50544a: We optimised the denoising parameters by performing a flare injection and recovery in synthetic light curves that replicated the noise profile of the CHEOPS instrument.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6bbfc254: The CHEOPS light curves were then denoised using a db18 wavelet and a decomposition level tailored to the number of data points in each orbit.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2a902fb4: Each flare was analysed by a breakdown algorithm to identify substructures.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9b284e32: We finally compared the flare populations detected in the original and denoised light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_05eecab6: After denoising, our sample was composed of 349 flaring events with energies ranging between $2.2 \times 10^{26}$ erg and $8.1 \times 10^{30}$ erg.
elements.2412-15297_sent_73d842e3: We identified a portion of $\sim$40\% of flares with a complex structure.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4259cda5: 123 complex flares were divided into individual components by using the flare breakdown algorithm.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c272225a: The denoising process significantly improved flare detection, increasing the overall recovery rate by $\sim$34\% and the identification of individual components of complex flares by $\sim$110\%.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b6cab73b: An improvement in recovery was observed across the entire energy range, but the highest gain was obtained for flares with energies inferior to $10^{29}$ erg.
elements.2412-15297_sent_92d03bd1: We identified several trends between flare parameters likely due to a detection bias, suggesting that the studied flare population is still not entirely recovered at low energies.
elements.2412-15297_sent_94442399: The limited sample size prevented us from establishing any clear relationship between flaring frequency and spectral type, or between the proportion of flaring stars and spectral type.
elements.2412-15297_sent_1b447b74: However, we observed a trend where the mean flare energy decreased from early to late M stars, while the mean equivalent duration increased. We constructed FFDs and applied power-law fits within the full recovery regime to determine the $\alpha$ indices.
elements.2412-15297_sent_11c441c0: For the complete flare sample, we obtained $\alpha$ = 1.92 $\pm$ 0.07, which is at the higher end of the values typically reported in the literature.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c1430867: We found no statistically significant differences in the $\alpha$ indices between the distributions of simple and complex flares or between flares from partially and fully convective stars.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6a967504: We also explored an alternative scenario to describe the observed flare distribution by fitting a log-normal distribution and comparing it to the traditional power-law.
elements.2412-15297_sent_87809aa1: The log-likelihood-ratio tests consistently favored the log-normal fit over the power-law.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a4215b42: However, the results lacked statistical confidence, as the p-value was consistently above 0.05.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5d30c32b: If flares were indeed better represented by a log-normal distribution, it could indicate that high-energy flares arise from the superposition of multiple weaker sympathetic flares erupting from nearby active regions.
elements.2412-15297_sent_3716760c: This shift could have strong implications for understanding the flare formation mechanisms, the impact of flares on exoplanetary atmospheres, and assessing long-term stellar activity.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2a70b681: Finally, we reviewed the main limitations of this study.
elements.2412-15297_sent_8130188a: First, the small sample size introduces considerable uncertainty in the derived flare statistics, leading to low confidence in the $\alpha$ indices extracted from the FFDs.
elements.2412-15297_sent_0fcc1ba0: Second, both the flare detection algorithm and the model used for flare injection and recovery were designed for regular flare studies and may bias denoising parameter selection, potentially favouring the recovery of regular flares over low-energy ones.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c1a201da: Third, although CHEOPS outperforms most other instruments in photometric precision and observing cadence, allowing it to detect flares with energies corresponding to the upper end of micro-flares, it still cannot capture all low-energy flares, creating a detection bias in the flare catalog due to detection incompleteness.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2ced432f: We propose several methods to mitigate these limitations and comment that upcoming missions, such as PLATO and the third mission extension of TESS, could significantly enhance low-energy flare statistics.

elements.2412-15297_sent_8b7c8611: Stellar flares are stochastic and intense bursts of electromagnetic radiation and charged particles that are triggered by magnetic reconnection in the chromosphere of stars.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2b98a8d4: This phenomenon occurs when the magnetic field lines in the chromosphere realign, which releases energy across the electromagnetic spectrum.
elements.2412-15297_sent_97dcf732: This can generate flares of varying intensity and duration and can significantly increase the stellar luminosity \citep{Benz_2010}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f09cfcab: The stellar magnetic activity significantly influences the frequency and energy of stellar flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2fe47915: M dwarfs are known for their high levels of magnetic activity and for flaring more frequently than earlier-type stars because their dynamo processes are more vigorous and yield a higher incidence of magnetic reconnection events \citep{Kowalski_2024}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_71d9efb8: These stars can experience more frequent and energetic flares that sometimes reach energy levels that are several orders of magnitude more intense than solar flares \citep{Benz_2010,Howard_2018}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d2377ceb: On the one hand, stellar flares can damage planetary atmospheres: X-ray and ultraviolet radiation, along with charged particles that are emitted during flares, can deplete atmospheric layers, especially on planets with weak magnetic fields or located near their host star.
elements.2412-15297_sent_7fbc018f: On the other hand, the energy that is radiated during stellar flares may play a role in triggering prebiotic chemical processes: The increased ultraviolet radiation during flares can trigger chemical reactions that are crucial for the emergence of life.
elements.2412-15297_sent_85b259a3: This suggests that flares may be necessary for prebiotic chemistry to happen on planets orbiting M dwarfs \citep{Scalo_2007,Rimmer_2018}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f50f1564: It is essential to understand the formation mechanisms, frequency, and energy distribution of stellar flares for several reasons.
elements.2412-15297_sent_86c82395: It provides insights into stellar magnetic activity and its variation with spectral type, especially in comparison to our Sun.
elements.2412-15297_sent_50fdacb4: By investigating the flare activity of various stellar types, it is additionally possible to refine models of stellar and planetary evolution \citep{Gudel_2007,Lammer_2012}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_56738af9: TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) and Kepler have provided valuable data on flare occurrences with cadences of minutes to a dozen seconds.
elements.2412-15297_sent_5302696a: These complex structures suggest that these non-classical flare shapes may result from emission cascades within a single active region or from sympathetic flares from neighbouring regions \citep{Davenport_2016,Howard_2022}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c9c921ad: In this context, the study of smaller-scale flare events becomes increasingly important for a better characterisation of stellar variability and the formation mechanisms of flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_63216c64: Extending photometric precision and observing cadence down to a few seconds could greatly improve our understanding of these phenomena and might reveal even finer flare structures.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b6e1cf45: This increased sensitivity may enable us to detect micro- and nano-flares, which are small-scale flaring events with energies ranging from $10^{22}$ to $10^{27}$ erg that are thought to play a crucial role in coronal heating \citep{Parker_1988}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_dbfcbef3: No concluding evidence supports micro- and nano-flares against other possible explanations, however, mainly because it is hard to gather precise statistics about these flares because they are hidden in the instrumental noise.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a58f11de: It is therefore still unclear whether micro- and nano-flares follow the same trends as more powerful flaring events.
elements.2412-15297_sent_fa1e5bb9: Furthermore, flare formation scenarios that diverge from self-organised criticality propose that flares might consist of an avalanche of several combined micro- and nano-flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_89d2772e: This would have implications for the current understanding of the impact of flares on exoplanetary atmospheres \citep{Audard2000,Sheikh_2016,Aschwanden_2019}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_91a171a3: The space telescope CHaracterising ExOPlanet Satellite (CHEOPS) is primarily designed to measure exoplanet transits.
elements.2412-15297_sent_1876bab9: With a precision of approximately 20 ppm over a 6-hour integration period for a V$\sim$9 star and down to 150 ppm for shorter timescales, CHEOPS surpasses other photometric instruments in the visible spectrum, such as TESS and current ground-based telescopes \citep{Benz_2021,Oddo_2023}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_e3d585b6: The exposure time of CHEOPS can also reach 0.001 seconds for very bright stars (V$\sim$6), which is several orders of magnitude faster than those of TESS and Kepler \citep{Borucki_2010,Ricker_2015}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_45ed0aa7: Although CHEOPS is limited to on-target observations, it provides high photometric precision and a fast accessible observing cadence.
elements.2412-15297_sent_1995bd68: These are crucial for capturing the short and stochastic nature of low-energy flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_88888a6f: To enhance the detection of flares in the CHEOPS light curves, denoising techniques based on the wavelet transform can filter out noise from the signal. The wavelet transform decomposes time-series data into different frequency components, which facilitates the isolation and reduction of noise.
elements.2412-15297_sent_21a1b1b7: Unlike the Fourier transform, which only provides frequency characteristics of the signal, the wavelet transform recovers both temporal and frequency information, making it more efficient in analysing transient signals such as stellar flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_a181c340: The discrete wavelet transform (DWT) is commonly used as a base for time-series denoising algorithms, and it has many applications outside of astronomy \citep{Pasti_1999,Polat_2018,Jang_2021}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_9063588e: The main downside of the DWT is that it introduces a downsampling of the input signal by definition, which can lead to a loss of details due to its non-redundant nature \citep{Dohono_1994}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_0bcea148: Although this involves a higher computational cost, the SWT provides the advantage of maintaining the original length of the signal at each decomposition level and providing a redundant, shift-invariant representation of the data \citep{Rhif_2019,Kumar_2021}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_d357d3f8: In the context of astronomy, techniques based on the SWT have been developed for noise filtering and demonstrated significant improvements in the signal-to-noise ratios (S/N) and the ability to recover faint astrophysical signals \citep{Strack_2006}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b5f76f5f: These methods emphasise the importance of adapting the current wavelet-based denoising techniques to the specific characteristics of astronomical time-series and show that these denoising algorithms could enhance the detection of faint transient phenomena such as low-energy flares.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f9fcd5d4: We explore the performance of the combination of the photometric precision of CHEOPS and an SWT-based denoising algorithm in enhancing the flare detection in M dwarf light curves.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c1cb863f: In Section \ref{section:methods} we describe the target selection, the data reduction, the SWT parameter exploration, the flare detection, and flare-breakdown algorithms.
elements.2412-15297_sent_edc79a7a: In Section \ref{section:results} we quantify the improvement in flare recovery that is achieved by denoising, we analyse the obtained flare catalogues, and we compare our results to the literature.
elements.2412-15297_sent_65e27451: In Section \ref{section:discussion} we discuss the limitations of this study and suggest several avenues for improvement.
elements.2412-15297_sent_c2459e7a: Among the data gathered by CHEOPS since the start of its operations, several observational programmes have targeted late-type stars.
elements.2412-15297_sent_2ec7eea5: Pagano) is a prominent programme that has been actively investigating the variability of late-K and M dwarf stars since the beginning of scientific observations in April 2020.
elements.2412-15297_sent_54ae77ae: This programme is particularly focused on characterising the photometric variability of these stars, including flares and star spots.
elements.2412-15297_sent_b8da3364: While its primary aim was to study the debris disks around various spectral types, this programme included observations of several late-type stars to explore the interaction between stellar activity and circumstellar dust.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6a1c4b4c: Szabó) was another significant programme, running from August 2022 to September 2023, and specifically targeted the young M dwarf star AU Mic.
elements.2412-15297_sent_749a55c8: The nominal observation mode of CHEOPS produces subarrays (200-pixel diameter) every few tens of seconds by co-adding multiple short sub-exposures onboard.
elements.2412-15297_sent_eead58df: In parallel, smaller cutouts called imagettes (60-pixel diameter) are downlinked at a higher cadence and can be used to construct light curves with finer temporal resolution.
elements.2412-15297_sent_017d1081: While individual sub-exposures can be as short as 0.001 seconds, the fastest cadence for downlinked subarrays is 22.65 seconds, set by telemetry and onboard processing constraints.
elements.2412-15297_sent_4a392478: In contrast, imagettes can be downlinked with cadences as short as 2.8 seconds.
elements.2412-15297_sent_6c06c657: All programmes mentioned above adopted a 3-second cadence for imagettes to balance S/N, avoid saturation, and maximise duty cycle \citep{Boldog_2023,Bruno_2024}.
elements.2412-15297_sent_f3e1215f: This cadence remains well-suited for detecting and analysing rapid, transient events like stellar flares and offers higher time resolution than the 20-second cadence of TESS.
